perturbation.original_text: MD Anderson and Adaptimmune shall comply with all federal, state, and local laws and regulations as well as ethical codes applicable to the conduct of each such Study ("Applicable Laws") to the extent, in each case, applicable to the relevant performance of a Party's obligations under this Agreement and any Study Order.
perturbation.changed_text: MD Anderson and Adaptimmune shall endeavor to comply with all federal, state, and local laws and regulations as well as ethical codes applicable to the conduct of each such Study (“Applicable Laws”) to the extent deemed necessary by each party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall comply' to 'shall endeavor to comply' weakens the obligation. Adding 'to the extent deemed necessary by each party' introduces subjective discretion, making compliance non-mandatory. This creates ambiguity and potential for non-compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 56.115 mandates that an IRB (Institutional Review Board) must ensure all research activities are conducted according to specific regulations to protect human subjects. The modified text contradicts this by allowing subjective discretion in following applicable laws, which could undermine the IRB's oversight and the mandated protections for study participants during clinical trials, opening the door to inconsistent application of safety protocols. The change introduces ambiguity around compliance with these regulations, making it difficult to enforce uniform safety standards during clinical studies.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: is conflicted. The IRB may decide whether an individual should remain in the room." 29. Does FDA expect the IRB chair to sign the approval letters? FDA does not specify the procedure that IRBs must use regarding signature of the IRB approval letter. The written operating procedures for the IRB should outline the procedure that is followed. 30. Does FDA prohibit direct communication between sponsors and IRBs? It is important that a formal line of communication be established between the clinical investigator and the IRB. Clinical investigators should report adverse events directly to the responsible IRB, and should send progress reports directly to that IRB. However, FDA does not prohibit direct communication between the sponsor and the IRB, and recognizes that doing so could result in more efficient resolution of some problems. FDA does require direct communication between the sponsors and the IRBs for certain studies of medical devices and when the 21 CFR 50.24 informed consent waiver has been invoked. Sponsors and IRBs are required to communicate directly for medical device studies under 21 CFR 812.2, 812.66 and 812.150(b). For informed consent waiver studies, direct communication between sponsors and IRBs is required under 21 CFR 50.24(e), 56.109(e), 56.109(g), 312.54(b), 312.130(d), 812.38(b)(4) and 812.47(b). IV. IRB Records 31. Are annual IRB reviews required when all studies are reviewed by the IRB each quarter? The IRB records for each study's initial and continuing review should note the frequency (not to exceed one year) for the next continuing review in either months or other conditions, such as after a particular number of subjects are enrolled. An IRB may decide, to review all studies on a quarterly basis. If every quarterly report contains sufficient information for an adequate continuing review and is reviewed by the IRB under procedures that meet FDA requirements for continuing review, FDA would not require an additional "annual" review. 32. 21 CFR 56.115(a)(1) requires that the IRB maintain copies of "research proposals reviewed." Is the "research proposal" the same as the formal study protocol that the investigator receives from the sponsor of the research? Yes. The IRB should receive and review all research activities [21 CFR 56.109(a)]. The documents reviewed should include the complete documents received from the clinical investigator, such as the protocol, the investigator's brochure, a sample consent document and any advertising intended to be seen or heard by prospective study subjects. Some IRBs also require the investigator to submit an institutionally-developed protocol summary form. A copy of all documentation reviewed is to be maintained for at least three years after completion of the research at that institution [21 CFR 56.115(b)]. However, when the IRB makes changes, such as in the wording of the informed consent document, only the finally approved copy needs to be retained in the IRB records. 33. What IRB records are required for studies that are approved but never started? When an IRB approves a study, continuing review should be performed at least annually. All of the records listed in 21 CFR 56.115(a)(1) - (4) are required to be maintained. The clock starts on the date of approval, whether or not subjects have been enrolled. Written progress reports should be received from the clinical investigator for all studies that are in approved status prior to the date of expiration of IRB approval. If subjects were never enrolled, the clinical investigator's progress report would be brief. Such studies may receive continuing IRB review using expedited procedures. If the study is finally canceled without subject enrollment, records should be maintained for at least three years after cancellation [21 CFR 56.115(b)]. V. Informed Consent Process 34. Is getting the subject to sign a consent document all that is required by the regulations? No. The consent document is a written summary of the information that s
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the mandatory nature of compliance with laws and ethical codes by changing 'shall comply' to 'shall endeavor to comply' and adding a subjective element ('to the extent deemed necessary by each party'). This directly contradicts the requirement for consistent application of regulations to protect human subjects during clinical trials, as emphasized by the Law Explanation referring to 21 CFR § 56.115. Although scraped snippets do not explicitly state the 'shall comply' language, Snippet 2 references 21 CFR 56.115(a)(1) regarding IRB maintenance of 'research proposals reviewed', which, as stated, includes protocols and consent documents. By introducing ambiguity, the revised language allows parties to subjectively decide the extent of compliance, undermining the IRB's oversight role and potentially leading to inconsistent application of safety standards, which the referenced regulation seeks to prevent.

perturbation.original_text: MD Anderson will promptly report any known deviation to Adaptimmune.
perturbation.changed_text: MD Anderson will attempt to report any known deviation to Adaptimmune.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will promptly report' to 'will attempt to report' weakens the obligation and makes reporting deviations discretionary. This is problematic because reporting deviations is essential for maintaining study integrity and patient safety.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 312.53 dictates the responsibilities of an investigator in a clinical trial, which include promptly reporting deviations from the approved protocol to the sponsor. The modification allows MD Anderson to merely 'attempt' to report deviations, which contradicts the requirement for prompt and mandatory reporting. This potentially hinders Adaptimmune's ability to oversee the trial effectively and take necessary corrective actions, risking patient safety and the validity of study data.

perturbation.justification: The modification from "will promptly report" to "will attempt to report" weakens the obligation to report deviations. 21 CFR § 312.53 (as cited in the Law Explanation, though the actual text of 312.53 is not present in the scraped snippets, only the title and surrounding sections of Part 312) dictates prompt reporting of deviations. 'Attempt' implies a lack of mandatory action, which contradicts the requirement for prompt and mandatory reporting necessary for study integrity and patient safety, as explained in the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Adaptimmune shall assume responsibility for reasonable medical expenses incurred by a Study subject for reasonable and necessary treatment if the Study subject experiences an illness, adverse event or injury that is a result of the Study Drug or any procedure required by the Protocol that the subject would not have undergone were it not for such Study subject's participation in the Study.
perturbation.changed_text: Adaptimmune may consider assuming responsibility for medical expenses incurred by a Study subject if they experience an illness, adverse event or injury potentially related to the Study Drug or procedure.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall assume responsibility' to 'may consider assuming responsibility' makes the assumption of responsibility optional. Replacing 'that is a result of' with 'potentially related to' introduces further ambiguity about causation, weakening Adaptimmune's financial obligation to cover necessary medical expenses.
perturbation.law_explanation: 45 CFR § 46.116(a)(6) requires that informed consent includes an explanation as to whether compensation and an explanation as to whether any medical treatment is available if injury occurs and, if so, what it consists of, or where further information may be obtained. By making the medical care coverage ambiguous ('may consider'), the agreement potentially fails to assure adequate medical treatment or compensation, effectively contradicting informed consent rules, and leaving study participants without guaranteed recourse if harmed during the study.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 45 CFR 46 | HHS.gov Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock A locked padlock ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Navigate to: 45 CFR 46 The HHS regulations for the protection of human subjects in research at 45 CFR 46 include five subparts. Subpart A, also known as the Common Rule, provides a robust set of protections for research subjects. The Common Rule, subpart A, was revised in recent years (the “2018 Requirements”). Subparts B, C, and D provide additional protections for certain populations in research. Subpart E provides requirements for institutional review board registration. Access the regulatory language using the links below. 2018 Requirements - official e-Code of Federal Regulation Exemptions - official e-Code of Federal Regulation Subpart B Additional protections for research with pregnant women and fetuses Subpart C Additional protections for research with prisoners Subpart D Additional protections for research with children Subpart E Requirements for IRB registration List of Expedited Categories (1998) In general, research initiated before January 21, 2019 continues to comply with the pre-2018 Common Rule unless the institution chose to transition it to the revised Common Rule. Pre-2018 Common Rule Content created by Office for Human Research Protections (OHRP) Content last reviewed February 11, 2025
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity regarding Adaptimmune's responsibility for medical expenses, weakening the obligation to cover necessary treatment. The original text states Adaptimmune 'shall assume responsibility', while the changed text states Adaptimmune 'may consider assuming responsibility'. This shift makes the coverage optional. The law explanation, referencing 45 CFR § 46.116(a)(6), states that informed consent must explain if compensation and medical treatment are available if injury occurs. By using 'may consider', the changed text creates an ambiguous situation failing to assure adequate medical treatment. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references § 46.116 as 'General requirements for informed consent'. The modification undermines the assurance of medical treatment, contradicting the requirement for informed consent as described in the law explanation.
